nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal marine waters, often on sandy or rubble substrates with moderate light
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; anchors via rhizoids into sand or rubble; photic zone of tropical and subtropical reef-adjacent habitats

avoidWith: tanks with no light schedule control

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing. Only known trade source for this species.

summary: Palm Tree Caulerpa is a slow-growing saltwater macroalgae with upright fronds that closely resemble miniature palm trees, making it one of the more ornamental Caulerpas available for reef displays. Like all members of the genus, it is considered allelopathic and a documented marine invasive outside its native range.
careNotes:
Palm Tree Caulerpa (Caulerpa paspaloides) is a siphonous green macroalgae notable for its upright fronds — each frond bears lateral pinnules arranged along a central stipe, giving it a feathery, arboreal appearance that sets it apart from other Caulerpa species kept in the hobby.

LIGHTING: Generally advised to provide high-intensity reef lighting (5,000K or higher, 8+ hours photoperiod). Like other display Caulerpa, moderate-to-high PAR appears to support healthy growth, though published PAR targets for this species specifically are not well-documented in the hobby literature.

ALLELOPATHY: As a member of Caulerpaceae, C. paspaloides is considered allelopathic — it has been documented in related species that caulerpin and caulerpenyne metabolites are released into the water column and can inhibit nearby corals and competing algae. Placement near sensitive SPS corals is generally not advised; maintaining physical separation and running activated carbon are commonly recommended by experienced reef keepers.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: Caulerpa species can undergo holocarpy — a reproductive event in which the organism converts its cytoplasm to gametes — typically triggered by stress (sharp nutrient crash, disrupted photoperiod, rapid temperature change). This effectively destroys the specimen and can cause an acute water-quality event (ammonia spike, oxygen depletion). Mitigations include regular harvesting to keep the colony in active growth, maintaining stable nutrient levels, and immediately removing any fronds showing pale/translucent reproductive coloration.

INVASIVE CONCERN: Caulerpa species are documented marine invasives. Captive specimens must never be released into natural waterways.

SHIPPING: Fronds may appear white or translucent upon arrival due to chloroplast displacement in darkness. This is normal and typically reverses within a few hours of light exposure.
breedingNotes: Palm Tree Caulerpa propagates readily through fragmentation. Cuttings that include at least one blade and a section of stolon tend to establish under appropriate lighting, and the plant also spreads naturally through vegetative stolon tip growth.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source for this species. Confirmed: palm-tree-like growth form, slower growth rate, display tank placement, invasive designation, California sales ban. Water parameters not specified — extrapolated from Caulerpa family care guidelines and sibling species C. prolifera.

additional.name: Caulerpa prolifera (AquaMate sibling entry sw-algae-004)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Caulerpaceae family care norms: water parameters, allelopathy caveats, holocarpy (going-sexual) risk, invasive status, and propagation methods. Same family and order; directly applicable husbandry baseline.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
